concepts.title: Geometric Progression and its expression
concepts.conceptBody:
Geometric Progression 

A geometric progression is a sequence of numbers where each term after the first is found by multiplying the previous term by a fixed number called the common ratio. The sequence $1,3,9,27$, .... is a geometric progression with first term 1 and common ratio 3 . The common ratio could be a fraction and it might be negative. In general we can write a geometric progression as follows :

geometric progression : a, ar, $\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{2}},\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{3}}$

where the first term is a and the common ratio is $r$.

Some important results concerning geometric progressions (G.P.) now follows :

The ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of a g.p. is given by $\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{\text{n}-1}}$. 

${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from the end 

The ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from the end of a finite G.P. consisting of $\text{m}$ terms is a ${{\text{r}}^{\text{m}-\text{n}}}$, where a is the first term and $r$ is the common ratio.

Note : If we are given the last term of G.P, say l, then the ${{n}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term from end is given by $l{{\left( \frac{1}{r} \right)}^{n-1}}$

concepts.title: Solved problems based on the definition of Geometric Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved problems based on the definition of Geometric Progression

If $a,b,c$ are in GP, then prove $\frac{{{a}^{2}}+ab+{{b}^{2}}}{bc+ca+ab}=\frac{b+a}{c+b}$ 

Solution : 

If $a,b,c$ are in GP, then

$\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{ar}$

and $\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{ar}^2$

Now, putting these values in LHS

L.H.S 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{{{a}^{2}}+a\left( ar \right)+{{(ar)}^{2}}}{\left( ar \right)\left( a{{r}^{2}} \right)+\left( a{{r}^{2}} \right)\left( a \right)+a\left( ar \right)} \\ {} & ~=\frac{{{a}^{2}}+{{a}^{2}}r+{{a}^{2}}{{r}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}{{r}^{3}}+{{a}^{2}}{{r}^{2}}+{{a}^{2}}r} \\ {} & ~=\frac{{{a}^{2}}\left( 1+r+{{r}^{2}} \right)}{{{a}^{2}}r\left( {{r}^{2}}+r+1 \right)}=\frac{1}{r} \\ \end{array}$

R.H.S. 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{\text{b}+\text{a}}{\text{c}+\text{b}}=\frac{\text{ar}+\text{a}}{\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{2}}+\text{ar}}=\frac{\text{a}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)}{\text{ar}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)} \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{\text{r}} \\ \end{array}$

L.H.S. = R.H.S. Hence proved

Illustration

In a finite GP the product of terms equidistant from the beginning and the end is always same and equal to the product of first and last term. 

Solution : 

Let ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}}\ldots {{a}_{n-1}},{{a}_{n}}$ be a finite GP with common ratio $r$.

$\mathrm{k}^{\text {th }}$ term from beginning $=\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{k}-1}$

$\mathrm{k}^{\text {th }}$ term from end $=\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{k}+1}$

$=a_n\left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\mathrm{k}-1}$

multiplying both the terms

${{a}_{k}}\cdot {{a}_{n-k+1}}={{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}\left( {{r}^{k+1}} \right){{\left( \frac{1}{r} \right)}^{k-1}}={{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}$

Hence proved.

Note : This can be taken as a standard result. 

Illustration 

If ${{\text{a}}^{\text{x}}}={{\text{b}}^{\text{y}}}={{\text{c}}^{\text{z}}}$ and $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ are in GP prove that $\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{b}}}\text{a}=\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{\text{c}}}\text{b}$. 

Solution : 

Given that ${{\text{a}}^{\text{x}}}={{\text{b}}^{\text{y}}}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$

Taking $\text{log}x\text{log}a=y\text{log}b=2\text{log}c$ (1)

also $\text{x},\text{y},\text{z}$ are in GP

then $\text{y}=\text{xr}\And \text{z}=\text{x}{{\text{r}}^{2}}$ (where ' $\text{r}$ ' is the common ratio)

from (i) $y \log b=2 \log c$

$x \log a=y \log b$ $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{xr} \log \mathrm{b}=\mathrm{xr}^2 \log \mathrm{c}$

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\log \mathrm{a}}{\log \mathrm{b}}$ (2) $\Rightarrow$ $r=\frac{\log b}{\log c}$ (3)

from (ii) & (iii) $\Rightarrow$ $\log _b \quad a=\log _c b$

$\frac{\text{log}a}{\text{log}b}=\frac{\text{log}b}{\text{log}c}$ $\left\{\because \frac{\log a}{\log b}=\log _b a\right\}$

concepts.title: Sum of a Geometric Progression (Geometric Series)
concepts.conceptBody:
Sum of geometric series 

The sum of first $n$ terms of a GP :

${{S}_{n}}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \frac{a\left( {{r}^{n}}-1 \right)}{r-1} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r\neq 1 \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ na }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r=1 \\ \end{array} \right.$

Case 1 : Suppose $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r=1$

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{a}+\text{a}+\text{a}+\ldots \ldots +\text{a}=\text{na}$

Case $2:$ If $\mathrm{r} \neq 1$

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ar}+\mathrm{ar}^2+\mathrm{ar}^3+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}-2}+\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}}$ (1)

$\mathrm{rS}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{ar}+\mathrm{ar}^2+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}-2}+\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}}$ (2)

So, now (1) - (2) gives

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{rS}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}}$

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}(1-\mathrm{r})=\mathrm{a}\left(1-\mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{n}}\right)$

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{n}}-1\right)}{(\mathrm{r}-1)}$

Special case when $n\to \infty $ (i.e. infinite number of terms)

If $\left| r \right|<1$ and $n\to \infty $ then $\left( \text{li}{{\text{m}}_{n\to \infty }}{{S}_{n}} \right)=\frac{a}{1-r}$

${{S}_{n}}=\frac{a\left( 1-{{r}^{n}} \right)}{1-r}$

Now $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ li}{{\text{m}}_{n\to \infty }}{{S}_{n}}=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{a}{1-r}-\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{n\to \infty }}\frac{\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{n}}}{1-r}$

$=\frac{a}{1-r}-0\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\underset{n\to \infty }{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,{{r}^{n}}=0 \right)=\frac{a}{1-r}$

concepts.title: Properties on Geometric Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of G.P. 

(1) If each term of a G.P. is multiplied (or divided) by some non-zero quantity the resulting progression is G.P. with some common ratio.

Suppose the G.P. is with ${{t}_{n}}=a{{r}^{n-1}}$

Now this GP is multiplied by some $\text{k}\left( \ne 0 \right)$

So G.P. will be

${{\text{t}}_{\text{n}}}=\left( \text{ak} \right){{\text{r}}^{\text{n}-1}}$

So, only the first term of G.P. has changed and the common ratio remains unaffected.

(2) If ${{\text{a}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}},\ldots $ and ${{\text{b}}_{1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}},\ldots $. be two G.P's of common ratio ${{\text{r}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{r}}_{2}}$ respectively, then ${{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}}$, $\ldots $ and $\frac{{{a}_{1}}}{{{b}_{1}}}\cdot \frac{{{a}_{2}}}{{{b}_{2}}}\ldots $. will also form G.P. common ratio will be ${{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}$ and $\frac{{{r}_{1}}}{{{r}_{2}}}$ respectively.

Let the series ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}}\ldots \ldots \ldots ..$. have the ${{n}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term as ${{a}_{1}}{{r}_{1}}{{~}^{n-1}}$ and the series ${{b}_{1}},{{b}_{2}}\ldots \ldots .$. have nth term as ${{b}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}{{~}^{n-1}}$

So the series ${{a}_{1}}{{b}_{1}},{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{2}}\ldots \ldots .$. will have ${{n}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term as $\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}{{\text{r}}_{1}}{{~}^{\text{n}-1}} \right)\left( {{\text{b}}_{1}}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{~}^{\text{n}-1}} \right)$

$=\left( {{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{1}} \right){{\left( {{\text{r}}_{1}}{{\text{r}}_{2}} \right)}^{\text{n}-1}}$

So the common ratio now becomes ${{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}$.

(3) If we have to take three terms in G.P. we take them as $\frac{\text{a}}{\text{r}}$, a, ar with common ratio $\text{r}$ and four terms as $\frac{\text{a}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}},\frac{\text{a}}{\text{r}},\text{ar},\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{3}}$ with common ratio ${{\text{r}}^{2}}$.

(4) If $a,b,c$ are in G.P then ${{b}^{2}}=ac$

(5) If ${{\text{a}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}},{{\text{a}}_{3}}\ldots \ldots ..{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$ is a G.P. then $\text{log}{{\text{a}}_{1}},\text{log}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\ldots \ldots .\text{log}{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$ is an AP (provided that all terms are non-zero & non-negative).

Why?

Now ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},{{a}_{3}}\ldots ..{{a}_{n}}$ form a G.P.

So $\mathrm{a}_1=\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{i}-1}$ (Let)

$\Rightarrow$ $\log a_i=\log a_1+(i-1) \log r$

This is clearly term of an AP

Q. How does $\text{log}{{a}_{1}}+\left( i-1 \right)\text{log}r$ represents an AP?

Ans. Let $\text{log}{{a}_{1}}=A$

And $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ logr}=\text{D}$ So, $\text{log}{{a}_{1}}+\left( i-1 \right)\text{log}r=A+\left( i-1 \right)D$

So, $\text{A}+\left( \text{i}-1 \right)\text{D}$ is term of an AP with first term as $\text{A}$ and common difference as $\text{D}$.

In a finite GP the product of the terms equidistant from the beginning and the end is always same and is equal to product of first & last terms.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{a}_{k}}{{a}_{n-k+1}}={{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}$ for $k=1,2,3\ldots n-1$

The reciprocals of the terms of a given GP, form a GP with common ratio $1/\text{r}$.

i.e. if ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}},{{a}_{3}}\ldots $ an are in GP with common ratio $r$ then $\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}},\frac{1}{{{a}_{2}}},\ldots \frac{1}{{{a}_{n}}}$ are in GP with common ratio $\frac{1}{\text{r}}$.

concepts.title: Solved Problems based on the Sum of Geometric Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved Problems based on the Sum of Geometric Progression

Q) Find the sum of the following series : 

$5+55+555+\ldots .n$ terms

Solution : 

These kind of questions can be solved with a little trick after which we get the result through GP.

We have $5+55+555+\ldots \ldots .\text{n}$ terms

$=5[1+11+111+\ldots \ldots . . \mathrm{n}$ terms

$=\frac{5}{9}[9+99+999+\ldots] \mathrm{n}$ terms

$=\frac{5}{9}[(10-1)+(100-1)+(1000-1) \ldots$.

$=\frac{5}{9}[(\underbrace{10+100+1000\ldots \text{n terms }}_{\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ This is in GP now } & {} \\ \text{ with a}=10 & {} \\ \end{array}})-\underbrace{\text{n}}_{\text{n times }}1]$

& $\mathrm{r}=10$

.$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ sum of series

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{5}{9}\left[ \frac{10\left( {{10}^{\text{n}}}-1 \right)}{\left( 10-1 \right)}-\text{n} \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{5}{9}\left[ \frac{10}{9}\left( {{10}^{\text{n}}}-1 \right)-\text{n} \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{5}{81}\left[ {{10}^{\text{n}+1}}-9\text{n}-10 \right] \\ \end{array}$

Q) If ${{S}_{n}}$ is the sum of first $n$ terms of a G.P. : $\left( {{a}_{n}} \right)$ and ${{S}_{n}}$ is the sum of another G.P.: $\left( 1/{{a}_{n}} \right)$, then show that : ${{\mathbf{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\mathbf{S}_{\text{n}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}{{\text{a}}_{1}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$. 

Solution : 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}={{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{a}}_{3}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}} \\ {} & \text{S}_{\text{n}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\frac{1}{{{\text{a}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{a}}_{2}}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +\frac{1}{{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}} \\ \end{array}$

For the first GP ${{a}_{n}}={{a}_{1}}{{r}^{n-1}}$

${{S}_{n}}=\frac{{{a}_{1}}\left( 1-{{r}^{n}} \right)}{1-r}$

where $\text{r}$ is the common ratio.

For the second GP $\left( \frac{1}{{{a}_{n}}} \right)$, common ratio $=\frac{1}{r}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & S_{n}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}}\frac{\left( 1-\frac{1}{{{r}^{n}}} \right)}{\left( 1-\frac{1}{r} \right)}=\frac{\left( {{r}^{n}}-1 \right)}{{{a}_{1}}\left( r-1 \right){{r}^{n-1}}}=\frac{{{r}^{n}}-1}{{{a}_{n}}\left( r-1 \right)} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & S_{n}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}}\frac{{{a}_{1}}\left( {{r}^{n}}-1 \right)}{r-1} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }S_{n}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }} & =\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}}{{S}_{n}} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & S_{n}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}}}{{S}_{n}} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{S}_{n}}=S_{n}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}{{a}_{1}}{{a}_{n}} \\ \end{array}$

Q) If ${{S}_{1}},{{S}_{2}}\ldots \ldots {{S}_{n}}$ are the sums of infinite geometric series whose first terms are $1,2,3,\ldots n$ and common ratios are $\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{4}\ldots \frac{1}{n+1}$ resp., then prove ${{S}_{1}}+{{S}_{2}}+\ldots {{S}_{n}}=\frac{1}{2}n\left( n+3 \right)$. 

Solution : 

We know the formula for an infinite series ${{S}_{\infty }}=\left( \frac{a}{1-r} \right)$ where a is the first term & $\text{r}$ is common ratio.

Here let ${{\text{S}}_{\text{k}}}=$ Sum of infinite series with first term

$\text{a}=\text{k }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ CR}=\frac{1}{\text{k}+1}$

$\therefore $ ${{\text{S}}_{\text{k}}}=\frac{\text{k}}{1-\frac{1}{\text{k}+1}}=\frac{\text{k}}{\frac{\left( \text{k}+1 \right)-1}{\text{k}+1}}$

$\Rightarrow $ ${{\text{S}}_{\text{k}}}=\text{k}+1$

$\therefore $ Putting $\text{k}=1,2,\ldots ..\text{n}$

$\sum \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{S}_1+\mathrm{S}_2+\mathrm{S}_3 \ldots \ldots \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}$

$=(1+1)+(2+1)+(3+1) \ldots(n+1)$

$=(1+2+3 \ldots \ldots n)+n \quad 1$

$=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}+\mathrm{n}$ $\{\because 1+2+3 \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n}$ is in AP with diff. $=1\}$

$=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+3)}{2}$

You can also learn this, sum of first $n$ natural nos. $=\frac{n\left( n+1 \right)}{2}$
